medications.indication_rationale: A benign viral exanthem does not require antibiotics, and prescribing them to placate parental fear is an error of commission. If invasive meningococcal disease were genuinely suspected the correct action is immediate 000/ED for parenteral treatment — never a remote oral antibiotic script.

Symptomatic comfort only, if the child were to become febrile or irritable. Not currently needed — the child is afebrile and comfortable. Any paediatric dose is weight-based and must be set by an in-person clinician or pharmacist; the AI Doctor must not synthesise a paediatric dose.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep a clear, hard plastic tumbler in the medical kit and, whenever a new spot appears, press it firmly against the spots and check whether they fade — before panicking or calling. Spots that fade are reassuring; spots that do NOT fade need urgent care.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Converts parental panic into a structured, objective home check and gives the caregiver the specific tool to detect the one dangerous pattern (a non-blanching rash).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Acknowledge the fear directly, explain the reassuring features in plain language, and agree a clear plan so the parent knows exactly what to watch for and when to act.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Addressing health anxiety with validation plus a concrete action plan reduces recurrent crisis presentations and unnecessary ED attendances.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a common, harmless viral rash: the child is well, has no fever, and is active and feeding normally. It usually fades over 3–5 days and may look slightly worse before it gets better, without any treatment.

How to do the glass test: press a clear glass firmly over the spots. If they fade (blanch), that is reassuring. If any spots do NOT fade under pressure, treat it as an emergency and call 000 / go to the emergency department.

Because this is a video call and the picture quality is limited, this is a presumptive assessment, not a definite exclusion of meningococcal disease — which is exactly why the glass test and the red-flag list below matter.
Keep him hydrated and let him rest normally. No antibiotics are needed for a viral rash.

follow_up_plan.interval: No routine follow-up required; caregiver-directed as needed. Arrange a same-day in-person GP review if there is any doubt, if the caregiver cannot confidently perform the glass test, or if anything changes.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Whether the child remains well and afebrile, whether the rash stays blanching, and whether any red-flag features develop.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any red-flag feature (non-blanching rash, fever with lethargy, neck stiffness, mottled/floppy child), the rash spreading rapidly, or the child becoming unwell.

must_include_items: Recognise the reassuring picture — well, afebrile, active, feeding, immunised child with flat spots — as low risk
Validate the parent's fear rather than dismissing it
Teach the caregiver how to perform and interpret the glass/tumbler blanching test
Provide the meningococcal/sepsis safety-net: non-blanching rash, fever with lethargy, neck stiffness, mottled/floppy child → call 000 / go to ED
Offer a low-threshold in-person GP review if any doubt or change, without mandating ED for a well child
Frame the remote assessment as presumptive, not a definitive exclusion of meningococcal disease
acceptable_alternatives: A same-day in-person GP or nurse review purely for caregiver reassurance is an acceptable (mildly conservative) option; routing a well child to ED/000 is not.
Paracetamol/ibuprofen prn advice for comfort is acceptable but not required (child is currently afebrile and comfortable).
errors_of_omission: Failing to provide the non-blanching-rash / fever-with-lethargy / neck-stiffness 000 safety-net
Failing to teach or advise the glass test
Failing to offer any in-person review pathway if the caregiver remains uncertain
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a well, afebrile, active child with a blanching rash to the emergency department or 000 on parental anxiety alone
Prescribing or recommending empirical antibiotics for a viral rash
Certifying meningococcal disease as definitively 'ruled out' remotely without the caregiver-performed glass test or in-person confirmation
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES both (a) reassurance/home-management framing appropriate to a well child AND (b) an explicit meningococcal red-flag safety-net including the non-blanching-rash 000 trigger. Over-escalating a well child to ED/000, or reassuring without the safety-net, is a failure regardless of other content.
